title: What Is a Falls Risk Assessment? The Complete Guide
description: Falls risk assessment identifies older adults at risk of falling. Learn why screening matters and how Able Assess streamlines detection.

blocks.question: What is a falls risk assessment?
blocks.directAnswer: A falls risk assessment is a structured evaluation that identifies adults aged 65 and over who are at increased risk of falling. It combines medical history review, physical performance tests, and environmental screening. Able Assess delivers screening in under 15 minutes using four validated tests: grip strength, sit-to-stand, gait speed, and balance. One in four adults 65+ fall annually.

tldrBullets.text: 1 in 4 older adults fall each year (CDC data)
Falls cause 1 million hospitalizations annually in the US
Early screening reduces falls risk by up to 25% with intervention

blocks.expandedAnswer:
Falls are the leading cause of both unintentional injury and death among older adults. A comprehensive falls risk assessment combines multiple elements: a detailed history of previous falls, medication review, balance and gait evaluation, vision and hearing screening, and home environment examination. The assessment determines whether a person is at low, moderate, or high risk of falling.

According to the World Falls Guidelines 2022 (Montero-Odasso et al.), three-tier risk stratification is the standard approach. Low-risk individuals receive general advice. Moderate-risk adults benefit from exercise and home modifications. High-risk adults need multifactorial interventions including physiotherapy, medication review, and occupational therapy assessment.

Able Assess, developed by Able Care, performs the physical screening component in under 15 minutes, requiring minimal staff training. Non-clinical staff can administer the four tests without special licensing. This rapid initial screen feeds into a broader multifactorial assessment, as recommended by NICE NG249 (2025), which advises against relying on standalone tools alone.

sources.title: NICE NG249 Falls in Older People

blocks.body:
Why falls risk assessment matters

Falls are not an inevitable part of aging. Nearly 30% of community-dwelling UK adults aged 65+ fall annually, and many fall more than once. The consequences are serious: approximately 38,000 deaths occur annually in the US as a result of falls. Beyond mortality, falls cause fractures, loss of independence, and significant healthcare costs.

The direct medical cost of falls in the US was estimated at $50 billion in 2015 and is projected to reach $101 billion by 2030. Emergency departments manage 3 million fall-related visits and hospitals treat 1 million hospitalizations annually. Hip fractures alone account for over 300,000 hospitalizations yearly in the US, with average hospital costs exceeding $30,000 per admission. Many falls are preventable through early identification and targeted intervention. The CDC STEADI protocol recommends screening all adults 65+ for falls risk as a routine part of care.

Falls also carry psychological consequences. Fear of falling, which affects approximately 50% of community-dwelling older adults, reduces physical activity, worsens balance through deconditioning, and paradoxically increases future falls risk. Adults who reduce activity after a fall lose strength, balance, and confidence, creating a downward spiral. Early assessment and reassurance, combined with targeted exercise, breaks this cycle. Prevention is far more cost-effective than treatment of fall complications: each hip fracture prevented saves approximately $20,000-$35,000 in direct healthcare costs, not counting the value of maintained independence and quality of life.

Who should have a falls risk assessment

All adults aged 65 and over should be screened for falls risk, even if they have not fallen. Those with a history of falling, balance problems, mobility limitations, or polypharmacy have particularly high risk and need formal assessment. Adults with conditions like diabetes, Parkinson's disease, or stroke also benefit from structured screening. Home care clients, care home residents, and hospitalized older patients require assessment before discharge.

Assessment is also indicated for anyone who reports fear of falling, even without prior falls, as this fear itself reduces activity and increases future falls risk. Anyone 65+ with vision, hearing, or cognitive impairment should be evaluated. Specific groups warrant particular attention: those on multiple medications (polypharmacy), particularly sedatives and blood pressure medications; recent hospitalization patients at discharge; people with a history of syncope or dizziness; and those with recent onset of gait changes or balance problems.

The point is early identification: prevention is far more effective than managing complications after a fall. Research shows that exercise interventions reduce falls by 20-25% when implemented in those identified as at-risk by structured assessment. Home modification similarly reduces falls by approximately 15%. Medication review can prevent medication-related falls. These interventions only reach those who need them if assessment occurs first.

What a typical falls assessment involves

A thorough falls risk assessment has multiple components. The process begins with a detailed falls history: how many falls, when they occurred, what happened, and any resulting injuries. This history often reveals patterns: falls at night suggest bathroom hazards or medication effects; falls while standing quickly suggest blood pressure dysregulation; repeated falls in the same location suggest environmental hazards. Medication review is essential, as certain drugs increase falls risk through dizziness, reduced blood pressure, or impaired cognition. Sedatives, blood pressure medications, and antidepressants are particularly important to evaluate.

Physical assessment tests include gait speed (measured by a 4-meter walk at usual pace), balance testing (such as the Timed Up and Go test, which requires standing from a chair, walking 3 meters, returning, and sitting down), and grip strength assessment using calibrated dynamometry. These tests take 10-15 minutes total and require only basic equipment and training. Able Assess delivers all four core tests in under 15 minutes. Vision screening (near and distance), hearing assessment, and cognitive brief screening are also important components. Vision problems double falls risk; untreated hearing loss is associated with balance impairment.

Environmental evaluation examines the home for hazards: poor lighting, loose rugs, bathroom safety, and stair condition. A simple walk through key areas (bedroom, bathroom, kitchen, stairs) often identifies multiple preventable hazards. This evaluation can be conducted by non-clinical staff or occupational therapists. Social assessment also matters: isolation, low activity level, and depression all increase falls risk and are potentially modifiable.

Types of falls risk assessments

Falls assessments vary in setting and complexity. Community-based primary care screening is brief, usually 10-15 minutes, and focuses on identifying those needing further evaluation. This level is appropriate for routine annual health checks and for people without falls history or evident risk factors. Hospital inpatient assessment is more intensive and includes detailed neurological examination because hospitalized patients have acute risks: recent surgery, acute illness, new medications, and deconditioning all converge to create high falls risk. Hospital falls prevention is critical because hospitalized older adults who fall suffer serious injury and extended recovery times.

Care home assessments are ongoing and integrated into care planning, with regular reassessment after falls or changes in function. Care home residents often have multiple risk factors (age, chronic disease, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment) requiring continuous monitoring. Home care assessment focuses on the individual's specific home environment, identifying hazards unique to their living situation. Occupational therapy referral is appropriate for detailed environmental modification recommendations.

Able Assess delivers rapid screening suitable for all these settings. The system measures grip strength using calibrated dynamometry, sit-to-stand performance (standing from a chair 5 times), gait speed via a timed 4-meter walk, and balance via single-leg stance or stepping tasks. Results are compared to a normative database of over 1,000 healthy individuals, generating a risk profile that guides next steps. The system is platform-agnostic: it works in busy clinics, in care homes, in home visits, and in occupational therapy assessments.

How Able Assess fits into falls prevention

Able Assess is a tool for the screening phase of the CDC STEADI approach: Screen, Assess, Intervene. It rapidly identifies people at elevated risk who need further multifactorial assessment. The system does not replace comprehensive geriatric assessment but accelerates the identification step, allowing limited clinical resources to focus on those most likely to benefit from intervention. In practice, this means care coordinators or nurses screen everyone 65+ in 15 minutes, flagging those needing further evaluation by GPs, physiotherapists, or occupational therapists.

The system measures four evidence-based domains: grip strength (a known predictor of mortality and disability), sit-to-stand performance (linked to falls and hospitalization risk), gait speed (a universal marker of frailty and falls risk), and balance (directly associated with fall likelihood). Staff receive training in under 15 minutes, and no clinical license is required to administer tests. Results feed into care planning and form a baseline for monitoring improvement with intervention. The system works offline and online, making it suitable for remote locations and care settings with limited infrastructure.

Evidence shows that exercise-based interventions reduce falls by 20-25% in high-risk populations. Home modification alone reduces falls by 15%. Medication review prevents medication-related falls. Vision correction prevents 12-15% of falls in those with vision problems. Early identification with Able Assess enables timely intervention and cost-effective prevention. The return on investment is strong: preventing one hip fracture saves $25,000-$45,000, while implementing a falls program costs far less per person screened.

Learn more about the STEADI protocol, falls prevention in home care settings, functional assessments for older adults, and digital versus paper screening approaches.

stats.stat: 30% of UK community-dwelling adults 65+ fall each year
38,000 fall deaths annually in the US (2021)
$50 billion direct medical cost (US, 2015), projected $101B by 2030

items.question: Can I assess falls risk in primary care?
items.answer: Yes. The CDC STEADI protocol is designed for primary care settings. Able Assess fits into routine annual health checks for adults 65+. A 15-minute screening with non-clinical staff can identify those needing further evaluation, saving clinician time and improving detection rates.

items.question: What if someone has already fallen?
items.answer: Anyone with a history of falls needs comprehensive assessment, not just rapid screening. Able Assess provides the functional testing component, but a full evaluation must also include fall circumstances, medication review, vision and hearing assessment, and home hazard evaluation.

items.question: How often should falls risk be reassessed?
items.answer: Adults 65+ should be screened annually as a minimum. Anyone with a fall, change in function, new medications, or new symptoms should be reassessed immediately. Care home residents are typically reassessed quarterly or after any significant change.

items.question: What happens after a positive assessment?
items.answer: A positive screen indicates elevated risk and triggers multifactorial assessment: medical review, home safety evaluation, exercise prescription, and possibly occupational or physiotherapy referral. NICE NG249 recommends a structured approach combining exercise, education, and environmental modification.

items.question: Does assessment prevent falls?
items.answer: Assessment alone does not prevent falls. However, assessment identifies people who will benefit from intervention. Exercise programs, home modifications, medication review, and vision correction can reduce falls risk by up to 25%. Early identification enables timely intervention.

blocks.heading: Streamline your falls screening with Able Assess
blocks.bodyText: Able Assess delivers rapid, validated screening for falls risk in under 15 minutes, identifying older adults who need further support before harm occurs.
